headlines.geopolitics: Community gives Navy veteran full military honors after no family claims remains
headlines.economy: DOJ creates new fraud division to tackle 'insane' problem of taxpayer theft
headlines.domestic: Senate moves toward passing sweeping housing bill despite political challenges

metadata.daily_do: Check on elderly neighbors or veterans in your community - sometimes the smallest gesture means everything

clips.caption: When Duke gets emotional about military honor 🇺🇸
Pringle drops historical knowledge about honoring the dead
Jax discovers the most Florida crime of all time

script.text: Look, I don't care if it's two dudes from Philly or the Pentagon — if you steal from taxpayers, you should go to prison. Period.
Good morning. I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction for Monday, March 10th. Let's get you caught up on what happened while you were sleeping.

Starting with geopolitics — or really, what it means to be American. Navy veteran Lonnie Wayman was buried yesterday at Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery with full military honors. The catch? He had no known family to claim his remains.
Oh wow.
Hundreds of veterans and community members showed up to make sure he wasn't buried alone. A VA rep said the word 'unclaimed' didn't fit what was happening — that the community was claiming him.
That's— that's beautiful, man.

On the economy, Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced the DOJ is creating an entirely new division to combat fraud. He called the problem quote 'insane' and cited examples like four million dollars stolen for fake homeless shelters in Minneapolis.
Four million for fake homeless shelters? That's like, advanced evil.

Two guys from Pennsylvania, according to Blanche. Just drove to Minneapolis and walked away with taxpayer money for shelters that didn't exist. The new division will appoint an assistant attorney general focused solely on fraud cases.
I mean, good. The oversight has been nonexistent.

And domestically, the Senate is moving toward passing comprehensive housing legislation. This is significant because housing affordability is expected to dominate the midterm elections, and historically, housing bills die in committee.
Key word being 'moving toward.' I'll believe it when I see it.
Fair point. Significant challenges remain that could derail the effort. We'll dig into all of this, but let's start with that story from Tennessee.
So Navy veteran Lonnie Wayman dies, no family comes forward, and instead of a quiet burial, hundreds of people show up. What does this tell us?
It tells us our veteran support systems are failing if people are dying alone in the first place. Like, how does someone who served their country end up with zero family connections?
Hold on, that's not necessarily a system failure. Some people just— life happens. Family dies, you move for work, relationships don't work out. The system here actually worked perfectly.
But shouldn't the VA be tracking this? Like, shouldn't there be case workers who—
The VA can't be everyone's family, Bree. But what happened here? The community stepped up. That's America working.
I hate that I'm about to sound serious for thirty seconds, but— Duke's right. This story isn't about failure, it's about people being decent humans.
Okay, but— and I love that hundreds of people showed up, that's beautiful— but why are we celebrating a Band-Aid instead of asking why we needed the Band-Aid?
What would you want the system to look like?
Better tracking of veterans, especially older ones. Regular check-ins. Some kind of— I don't know, buddy system? Social workers who specialize in veteran isolation?
So more government bureaucracy to solve a problem that the community already solved voluntarily. Got it.
It's not bureaucracy, it's— okay, fine, it's bureaucracy, but it's bureaucracy that prevents people from dying alone!
Can we just agree that everyone involved here is good people and move on before I start crying at ten AM?
The VA representative said something interesting — that 'unclaimed' was the wrong word because the community was claiming him. What's the difference?
The difference is honor. Family claims you by blood. Community claims you by choice. Both matter, but the choice one— that hits different.
That's actually beautiful.
I mean, when I deployed, the guys in my unit— some of them I was closer to than my own cousins. Military family is real family.
And that extends beyond active service.
Exactly. You serve, you're part of something forever. Doesn't matter if you're twenty-two or ninety-two.
Okay, that's— I can get behind that. I still think we need better systems, but I can get behind the community piece.
Plot twist: Bree and Duke agree on something. Mark your calendars, folks.
Alright, let's pivot to fraud. The DOJ is creating an entire new division because, in Todd Blanche's words, the fraud problem is quote 'insane.' Bree, you mentioned oversight earlier.
Yeah, the examples he gave are wild. Four million for fake homeless shelters? School lunch programs that don't exist? Like, how does money just— flow out with no verification?
Because the government throws money around like confetti and then acts shocked when people pick it up. This is exactly why I don't trust big spending programs.
But the answer isn't to stop helping people, it's to— to actually check where the money's going!
Right, which is what this new division is supposed to do. I'm just saying, maybe we should have thought of that before we sent billions to Minneapolis.
Blanche said they want to prosecute anyone who steals over a thousand dollars. That's— that's a lot of prosecutions.
A thousand bucks? Dude, that's like, three months of my grocery budget. These guys are stealing millions and we're like 'eh, boys will be boys.'
Look, I don't care if it's two dudes from Philly or the Pentagon — if you steal from taxpayers, you should go to prison. Period.
I agree with the enforcement part, but we also need to fix the systems that make this fraud so easy in the first place.
Finally, something we agree on. Though I bet we disagree on how to fix it.
Probably. I'm thinking better technology, more caseworkers, real-time verification systems—
And I'm thinking smaller programs, local control, and basic common sense checks.
Both approaches could work. The question is whether Congress will fund either one.
Professor, when societies honor their military dead, especially those without families, is there historical precedent for what we saw in Tennessee?
Oh my word, yes. This touches something ancient and universal. Let me tell you about the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier, which began after World War One.
Britain, France, America — they all faced the same problem. Hundreds of thousands of dead soldiers, many unidentifiable. Families with no grave to visit, no closure to find.
Oh man, that's heavy.

The British started it. They selected one unidentified soldier from the Western Front and buried him in Westminster Abbey with full state honors. The idea was that every family could believe it might be their boy.
That's— wow. That's actually brilliant.

America followed suit in nineteen twenty-one. But here's what's fascinating — these weren't government initiatives initially. They came from mothers' groups, veterans' organizations, ordinary citizens demanding their dead be honored.
Just like Tennessee.
Precisely. The impulse to honor military sacrifice transcends bureaucracy. It's primal, civic, sacred. When the state fails to act, communities step forward.
In ancient Rome, soldiers who died without family were buried by their legion. The unit became their family. Same principle — military service creates bonds that death cannot break.
So this isn't about fixing a broken system, it's about honoring an ancient tradition.
The system isn't broken, it's working exactly as it should. Community action, voluntary association, civic virtue — that's how healthy societies function.
But Professor, what about preventing the isolation in the first place?

A worthy goal, my dear. But even in the tightest communities throughout history, some souls have always walked alone. The measure of a society isn't whether that happens, but how it responds when it does.
That's exactly right.
Tennessee responded beautifully. Hundreds of strangers becoming family for a man they never met, bound only by shared citizenship and respect for service. It's democracy at its finest.
Professor, you just made me feel better about humanity. That's not supposed to happen on a Monday.
Ha! Well, occasionally history provides hope instead of horror. We should celebrate when it does.

Alright everybody, time for Rapid Fire, where we process the week's weirdest news so you don't have to. First up: a boss in Ireland repeatedly shouted 'potato' at his Irish employee as workplace harassment.
I'm sorry, what?
Yeah, like, that was his whole harassment strategy. Just yelling 'potato' at people. This is why HR exists, folks.
That's— that's not even creative harassment. That's just stupid harassment.
I have so many questions and I want answers to none of them.
Next: Palantir's AI weapons system has been reassigned from, uh, weapons stuff to finding office chairs for government workers.
Wait, we're using military AI to buy furniture?
Apparently the AI that can identify enemy targets can also identify really good deals on ergonomic seating. It's called diversification, Duke.
Swords into plowshares, as the Bible says. Though I suspect Isaiah wasn't thinking about office furniture.
Honestly, this might be the best use of military technology I've heard in years.
Louisiana schools must display the Ten Commandments in classrooms by Friday. So if you're a teacher in Baton Rouge, you've got four days to figure out poster logistics.
That's going to court so fast it'll make your head spin.
I mean, thou shalt not steal is pretty relevant given our fraud discussion.
And finally, the most Florida story of all time: a drug dealer locked his customer in a storage unit for months over unpaid debt.
Months?
Months! Like, this guy was basically running a debtor's prison out of a Public Storage facility. The entrepreneurial spirit of America, everybody.
That's not entrepreneurship, that's kidnapping.
Well yeah, but it's very organized kidnapping. He probably had spreadsheets.
I need everyone to stop talking so I can process what I just heard.
You know, debtors' prisons were common in colonial America. Though they usually didn't involve drug dealers and storage units.
See? Everything old is new again. That's this week's Rapid Fire, folks. America: we're doing great.
Alright, time for your Daily Do. After hearing about Lonnie Wayman and that incredible turnout in Tennessee, here's what you can do today.
Check on an elderly neighbor or veteran in your community. Not because they're dying — just because they're living. Sometimes the smallest gesture means everything.
That's beautiful, Leo. My neighbor Mr. Kim is ninety-three and I see him getting his mail every day. I should probably actually say hello.
There's also— if you want to do more, most cities have veteran outreach programs that need volunteers. You don't have to be a veteran to help.
And if you're socially awkward like me, just wave. Waving counts. Don't overthink it.
The point is connection. Real human connection. It doesn't have to be complicated.
As the Roman philosopher Seneca wrote, 'Every new beginning comes from some other beginning's end.' Sometimes we're the beginning someone else needs.
That's your Daily Do. Be someone's beginning today. We'll be back tomorrow with more news and probably more weird Florida crimes.
I'm already researching storage unit regulations for tomorrow's show. This is my life now.
I'm Leo Barros. Thanks for listening to The Friction.
